Author: AiJW
Title: Context Pollution: Why Your AI Coding Agent Goes Rogue & The Exact Steps to Fix It
Text:
Context Pollution: Why Your AI Coding Agent Goes Rogue & The Exact Steps to Fix It
Your Cursor agent was perfect for 20 minutes. Then it started mixing Python into your TypeScript tests. Here’s what’s actually happening — backed by research — and the field-tested fixes ranked by confidence level.
Then it edits a file you never mentioned. It re-suggests a fix you already rejected. It generates requests.get()
inside a Playwright browser test. It's importing from jira import JIRA
into your TypeScript file.
This isn’t hallucination. It’s a well-documented engineering phenomenon with research-backed causes and — crucially — field-tested fixes.
I spent weeks digging through research papers, engineering blogs, and practitioner war stories to build a definitive guide. Every fix below is tagged with a confidence level so you know exactly what’s battle-tested versus what’s promising-but-theoretical.
What’s Actually Happening (In 60 Seconds)
Three overlapping phenomena are killing your agent’s performance:
ProblemPlain EnglishSourceContext RotModel accuracy degrades as tokens pile up — even on simple tasksChroma Research, July 2025 — tested 18 LLMsContext PollutionCorrections, error logs, side-quests dilute your real instructionsKemple, July 2025 — cosine-similarity frameworkAgent DriftAgent’s outputs become its own future inputs, creating a feedback loopRath, arXiv Jan 2026 — ~50% drift by 600 turns
“LLMs, like humans, lose focus at a certain point. Context must be treated as a finite resource with diminishing marginal returns.” — Anthropic Engineering, Sept 2025
The Evidence (Show Me the Data)
Chroma Research: Performance Drops Below 50% at 32K Tokens
Hong, Troynikov, and Huber (July 2025) tested 18 frontier LLMs on tasks requiring semantic understanding — not simple keyword retrieval. Models that scored 90%+ on short prompts dropped below 50% accuracy at 32K tokens. GPT-4.1 started inserting random lowercase text. Gemini generated unrelated fragments. Claude decayed slowest but began refusing to answer on longer tasks.
⚠️ Honesty check: Chroma’s tests used synthetic benchmarks, not actual IDE sessions. The extrapolation to “your Cursor agent at turn 25” is plausible and consistent with practitioner reports, but not directly proven in IDE workflows.
The Autoregressive Poison Loop
Rath’s Agent Drift paper (arXiv, Jan 2026) identified reinforcement through autoregression: in multi-turn sessions, the agent’s outputs become its own future inputs. Early mistakes get encoded and increasingly bias future generations. This is why correcting a drifting agent mid-session often makes things worse — your correction adds more tokens to an already polluted window.
Distractors Are Deadlier Than Random Noise
Shi et al. (2023) proved LLMs are “easily distracted by irrelevant context.” Chroma extended this: topically related distractors are far more damaging than random noise. In your IDE, error logs from a previous attempt, code from adjacent files, and conversation about rejected approaches are exactly these kind of potent distractors — on-topic enough to hijack the model’s attention.
JetBrains at NeurIPS 2025: Masking Beats Summarization
Presented at the Deep Learning 4 Code workshop (Dec 2025): selectively hiding tool outputs while preserving the reasoning chain produced better results than compressing everything into a summary. Since IDE agent turns are dominated by file contents and test output, this finding has direct implications for how compaction should work.
Spot the Drift: Early Warning Cheatsheet
Don’t wait until the agent is generating Jira imports in your test file:
🚨 Critical Rule: When you see drift, do NOT try to correct the agent in the same session. Every correction adds more tokens to a polluted context. Start fresh.
The Fixes: Ranked by Confidence Level
This is the part most articles skip. Every technique is tagged:
- ✅ Proven — Documented in official tools + confirmed by multiple independent practitioners
- ⚠️ Strong Consensus — Recommended by experts, logically grounded, no controlled study
- 🔬 Experimental — Promising framework, no production tooling yet
The Code: Copy-Paste Ready
Claude Code: Manual Compact + Re-anchor
# Compact at logical breakpoints:
/compact Summarize: decisions made, files changed, open TODOs only
# After compaction, force re-read (GitHub issue #14258):
Read CLAUDE.md and tests/app-a/catalog.spec.ts before continuing.
Current task: Add filtering tests for product catalog.
Language: TypeScript ONLY. Framework: Playwright ONLY.
Cursor: Rules That Survive Sessions
# .cursor/rules/playwright-tests.mdc
---
description: Playwright test conventions for all test files
globs: tests/**/*.spec.ts
---
- Language: TypeScript. Never generate Python.
- Use getByRole() and getByTestId(). Never CSS selectors.
- Use expect() from @playwright/test. Never assert.
- One test file per page/feature. Max 10 tests per file.
Mid-Session Anchoring (Any Tool, Every ~10 Turns)
STATUS CHECK:
- Task: App A E-Commerce Playwright tests
- File: tests/app-a/filter.spec.ts
- Language: TypeScript ONLY
- Next: Add test for category dropdown filtering
- Constraint: No API mocking — use real staging environment
Session Handoff (Before Ending Any Session)
## Completed
- catalog.spec.ts: 8 tests passing
- cart.spec.ts: 5 tests passing
## In Progress
- filter.spec.ts: product filtering by category
## Decisions
- Using storageState for auth (not per-test login)
## Next Session
- Complete filter tests → checkout flow
Real Scenario: The Multi-App QA Session
Setup: QA lead, 20+ apps on EC2/VPN, Cursor agent mode, Playwright tests.
❌ What Goes Wrong (Without Context Hygiene)
- Turn 1–10: Write Playwright tests for App A (React). Agent is perfect.
- Turn 11–15: Quick check on App B’s Jira ticket + Python API. Seems harmless.
- Turn 16–20: Back on App A. Agent mixes Python
assert()
into TypeScript. - Turn 21–25: Agent references App B’s API endpoints in frontend tests.
- Turn 26+: Full drift. Every correction makes it worse. Session is dead.
Why: App B’s Python/Jira context became a topically-related distractor that hijacked the model’s attention.
✅ What Should Happen (With Context Hygiene)
- Per-app
.cursor/rules/
files define language, framework, conventions - App B check happens in a separate chat tab (
Cmd+L
) - Only the relevant Jira requirement is pasted back into App A’s session
- Every ~10 turns: STATUS CHECK re-anchors task + constraints
- Before context limit: handoff saved to
.claude/handoff-*.md
- New session reads: handoff + rules + current test file only
Result: Zero cross-app pollution. Agent stays on-task across session boundaries.
Enterprise Pattern: Sub-Agent Isolation
For teams managing multiple applications, Manus applies a Go concurrency principle:
“Share memory by communicating, don’t communicate by sharing memory.” — Phil Schmid, Dec 2025
- Orchestrator: Holds test plan, routes tasks. Sees only the plan.
- Per-app sub-agents: Each operates with only that app’s context. Zero cross-pollination.
- Summary-only returns: Pass/fail + coverage %. No conversation history crosses back.
This is how Claude Code subagents work — isolated context windows returning only summaries. Anthropic’s multi-agent research system outperformed single-agent approaches using this pattern.
The One Rule
Fresh context beats long context. Always.
Short, focused sessions with explicit file references will always outperform long conversations where you hope the agent “remembers.”
When in doubt, start a new session.
